entries.position_summary: The country supports negotiating a Fissile Material Cut-Off Treaty in Geneva. It views such a treaty as a necessary step to prevent future nuclear arms racing.
entries.quote: And, here in Geneva, we continue to advocate for the negotiation of a Fissile Material Cut-Off Treaty, a step needed to help stop future arms racing in its tracks.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its Article VI obligation to pursue good-faith negotiations on nuclear disarmament and commits to doing so through bilateral treaties, multilateral fora, and practical actions to advance NPT disarmament goals.
entries.quote: The United States stands by our obligation under the NPT to pursue negotiations in good faith on effective measures on nuclear disarmament -- in bilateral treaties, through multilateral fora, and actions to advance NPT disarmament goals.

entries.position_summary: The country urges Russia to return to compliance with New START and to engage in talks to manage nuclear risks. It supports negotiating a post-2026 nuclear arms control framework.
entries.quote: It is time for Russia to return to compliance with New START and engage with us to manage nuclear risks and discuss a post-2026 nuclear arms control framework.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores its commitment to transparency by voluntarily releasing aggregate New START data in May 2023. It criticizes Russia for not providing comparable data under the treaty.
entries.quote: Moreover, the United States voluntarily released aggregate data on U.S. strategic offensive arms under the New START Treaty on May 15, 2023. We note that Russia did not.

entries.position_summary: The statement condemns Russia’s war against Ukraine and its irresponsible nuclear rhetoric, criticizes reckless actions at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant as endangering nuclear facilities and safety, and rejects Russia’s claimed suspension of New START as contrary to international law.
entries.quote: Russia’s unprovoked war against Ukraine tragically continues, as do Russia’s irresponsible nuclear rhetoric, its reckless actions at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant, and its claim to suspend the New START Treaty, a claim that is inconsistent with international law.

entries.position_summary: The country supports arms control engagement, risk reduction measures, and advancing a fissile material cutoff treaty to strengthen the NPT. It also stresses the importance of negative security assurances and calls on all states to uphold the assurances they have given.
entries.quote: Steps such as these – on arms control engagement, on risk reduction, and on Cutoff – can help strengthen the NPT’s role as a positive force for restraint and diplomacy. So too do negative security assurances, and we call on all states to uphold the assurances they have given.

entries.position_summary: The country calls for using the NPT review process to advance nuclear restraint through renewed arms control and concrete risk-reduction measures. It insists on full compliance with IAEA safeguards and supports expanding peaceful nuclear uses globally.
entries.quote: At a time of rising instability and risk, let’s use this review process to advance policies and practices of nuclear restraint, including action on arms control and risk reduction, insisting on full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and facilitating an expansion of peaceful uses on a global scale.

entries.position_summary: The delegation condemns Syria’s safeguards non-compliance, the DPRK’s unchecked nuclear and ballistic advances, and Russia’s seizure of Ukraine’s Zaporizhzhia NPP, urging accountability for actions undermining the nonproliferation regime. It criticizes Russia and the PRC for expanding and diversifying their nuclear arsenals while showing little interest in arms control, and warns about destabilizing coordination among Russia, the PRC, the DPRK, and Iran.
entries.quote: agreement, Syria’s non-compliance with its comprehensive safeguards agreement, the DPRK’s unchecked advances of its nuclear and ballistic weapons programs, and Russia’s seizure of Ukraine’s Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant. Furthermore, Russia and the PRC are expanding and diversifying their nuclear weapons arsenals — showing little or no interest in arms control. Russia, the PRC, and the DPRK, together with Iran, are increasingly cooperating and coordinating with each other in ways that run counter to peace and stability and exacerbate regional tensions. We will have more to say on these topics in our statement on regional issues; however, my delegation wishes to stress that part of keeping the nonproliferation regime strong is holding those whose actions contradict it accountable.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports negotiating an FMCT, has engaged in UNGA FMCT initiatives, and maintains a unilateral moratorium on producing fissile material for weapons while urging China to follow suit. It cites irreversible disarmament steps—dismantling plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions via arms control with Russia, and fissile material disposition—and endorses the principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. The U.S. advances verification capabilities through the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE.
entries.quote: We will also engage constructively in the other subsidiary bodies, including on the Cessation of the nuclear arms race and nuclear disarmament. In addition to our efforts at the CD, the United States actively participated in two UN General Assembly FMCT initiatives in 2014-2015 and 2017-2018 that we believe laid important groundwork for future FMCT negotiations. We continue to maintain our unilateral moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and urge the PRC – the only Nuclear Weapon State that has not done the same – to join us. 2000 Steps Five and Thirteen; 2010 Actions Two and Eighteen: Irreversibility and Further Development of Verification Capabilities: On Steps Five and Thirteen, the United States has undertaken important steps to support the principal of irreversibility, including through irreversible dismantlement of all weapons-grade plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions through arms control treaties with Russia, disposition of fissile material from nuclear weapons, and other efforts. We also support language from the 2022 RevCon Draft Final document that acknowledges the mutually reinforcing principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. Active U.S. engagement in the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE underscore our commitment to engaging a broad set of countries on the development of future tools for arms control that support irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency in nuclear disarmament. We also welcome the group statement on irreversibility in nuclear disarmament delivered by Norway,

entries.position_summary: The United States expresses readiness to engage in bilateral arms control discussions with Russia and China without preconditions, signaling openness to negotiate future arms control arrangements.
entries.quote: That is why National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan told the world the United States is willing to engage in bilateral arms control discussions with Russia and with China – without preconditions.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes China for refusing to engage in bilateral risk-reduction and arms-control discussions, noting Beijing’s suspension of their bilateral consultations. It implies a desire for such engagement that is not being reciprocated by the PRC.
entries.quote: PRC continues to show no interest in engaging bilaterally on risk reduction or arms control, as demonstrated by its suspension of our bilateral consultations.

entries.position_summary: The country urges Russia and China to engage in arms control and risk-reduction discussions. It offered to negotiate a post‑February 2026 arms control framework without preconditions, but states that Russia has refused to participate.
entries.quote: Over the past two years, we have pressed Russia and the PRC to engage on arms control and risk reduction, including by urging Russia to work with us to develop a post-February 2026 arms control framework, which it has refused to do. We offered to do so without preconditions.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its commitment to disarmament consistent with the Thirteen Steps and Article VI, stating readiness to pursue critical arms control measures. It seeks to reduce nuclear risks through bilateral engagement with Russia and China and via the P5 process, despite their current unwillingness to engage.
entries.quote: The “Thirteen Steps” clearly identified the need for all Nuclear Weapon States to engage in the disarmament process. It is because of the seriousness with which we take our disarmament-related commitments that President Biden made clear at the UN last September that “no matter what else is happening in the world, the United States is ready to pursue critical arms control measures.” It's also why we will continue to seek to reduce nuclear risks with Russia and the PRC bilaterally and through the P5 process, despite their unwillingness to engage.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency as a priority, using it to support arms control efforts and to build confidence among NPT parties. It views openness as a means to strengthen mutual trust and the effectiveness of arms control.
entries.quote: Finally, the United States prioritizes transparency, whether as a tool of arms control or to build confidence among NPT parties.

entries.position_summary: The country condemns Russia’s actions in Ukraine, accusing it of seizing peaceful nuclear facilities and engaging in reckless nuclear rhetoric. It also asserts that Russia has violated provisions of the New START Treaty.
entries.quote: • Russia, an NPT nuclear-weapon state and treaty depository, is engaged in an illegal war against its non-nuclear weapon state neighbor. It has violently seized Ukraine’s peaceful nuclear facilities; engaged in reckless nuclear rhetoric; violated provisions of the New START Treaty; and may be

entries.position_summary: The statement condemns Russia for qualitatively expanding its nuclear forces, undermining arms control by purporting to suspend New START, and engaging in irresponsible nuclear rhetoric. It also accuses Russia of violating its security assurances to Ukraine.
entries.quote: Russia has developed novel nuclear weapons and improved dual-capable delivery systems, unlawfully purported to suspend the New START Treaty, engaged in irresponsible nuclear rhetoric, and reneged on the security assurances it provided Ukraine.

entries.position_summary: The statement condemns Russia for violating its security assurances to Ukraine under the Budapest Memorandum and criticizes its purported suspension of New START and irresponsible nuclear rhetoric. It argues these actions increase nuclear risks and the chances of miscalculation, contrasting them with P3 efforts to engage responsibly on negative security assurances.
entries.quote: Unfortunately, in contrast to P3 efforts to engage responsibly on negative security assurances, the Russian Federation has disregarded the commitments and assurances it made in the Budapest Memorandum to respect the independence, sovereignty, and territorial integrity of Ukraine. Russia’s legally invalid, purported suspension of the New START Treaty and irresponsible rhetoric regarding nuclear weapons not only increase nuclear risks, but also heighten the possibility of miscalculation.

entries.position_summary: The statement credits the NPT with containing proliferation, establishing robust IAEA safeguards, and advancing disarmament through stockpile reductions and arms control. It also praises the spread of nuclear-weapon-free zones and the benefits delivered by peaceful nuclear applications.
entries.quote: Since it entered into force, we have contained nuclear proliferation; developed robust international safeguards to monitor peaceful nuclear programs; made significant steps towards disarmament through stockpile reductions and other arms control measures; seen entire regions adopt nuclear-weapon-free zones; and, through the peaceful uses of nuclear energy, science, and technology, improved the lives of millions.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear build-up, questioning the credibility and clarity of its no-first-use policy and broader doctrine. It notes Beijing’s reluctance to engage substantively and expresses skepticism about a proposed no-first-use treaty, particularly regarding how it would be verified. Overall, it views China’s initiative as lacking substance.
entries.quote: The PRC’s actions, in particular its rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up, raise questions about Beijing’s already ambiguous, stated “no first use” policy and its nuclear doctrine more broadly, calling into question what such an initiative aims to achieve. The PRC, to date, has resisted substantive engagement to answer these questions. We also continue to have concerns about how its proposed no first use treaty would operate in practice, including with respect to verification. We see more style than substance.

entries.position_summary: The United States denies responsibility for destabilizing actions and implicitly condemns Russia for invading a neighbor, undermining decades of bilateral arms control progress, engaging in dangerous nuclear rhetoric, endangering civilian nuclear facilities, and moving nuclear weapons closer to others’ borders in the context of the war in Ukraine.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, It is not the United States that has attacked a neighboring country without provocation. It is not the United States that has undermined a half century of progress on bilateral arms control, engaged in irresponsible and dangerous nuclear rhetoric, endangered civilian nuclear energy facilities, or moved nuclear weapons closer to others’ borders.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores the importance of the NPT and commits to collaborating with other states to reduce and eliminate nuclear threats. It pledges to pursue risk reduction and arms control efforts that enhance the security of the U.S., its allies, and partners.
entries.quote: In our main Cluster I statement, the United States reaffirmed its commitment to enhancing global security and working with other states towards the mutual goal of reducing and eliminating nuclear threats. We stressed the importance of the NPT in that regard. In line with President Trump’s stated objectives, we will pursue risk reduction and arms control efforts that advance U.S., allied, and partner security.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes China for expanding its nuclear arsenal while withholding basic information on stockpiles, fissile materials, and nuclear posture. It argues China’s lack of transparency and refusal to join arms control efforts undermines its commitment to NPT objectives and the broader nonproliferation regime.
entries.quote: Meanwhile, China refuses to disclose even the most basic information about its growing stockpile size, fissile material holdings and production, or to answer basic questions regarding its nuclear posture. Its actions, lack of transparency, and especially its refusal to join arms control efforts raise serious questions about its commitment to the NPT’s objectives, undermining the global nonproliferation regime.

entries.position_summary: The country signals intent to engage on arms control, nuclear risk reduction, and effective verification, prioritizes full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and supports expanding access to peaceful uses of nuclear technology ahead of the next Review Conference.
entries.quote: Ahead of next year’s Review Conference, there is much to be done. Over the next year, we look forward to engaging on ideas for arms control, reducing nuclear risk, and effective verification; on ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards; and on expanding access to peaceful uses. As Secretary of State Rubio has repeatedly said, “peace is not a noun, it is a verb.” So, let’s get to work.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges the P5 to pursue multilateral arms control and risk-reduction measures to prevent nuclear war and avoid arms races. As P5 Chair, it convened multiple expert-level meetings in 2023 to advance these efforts.
entries.quote: In parallel, the five nuclear weapons states must continue to engage in multilateral arms control and risk reduction efforts; they have an urgent responsibility to work together to prevent nuclear war and avoid arms races. As the P5 Chair, the United States has held multiple expert level meetings in 2023 to that end.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China for expanding its nuclear forces in ways seen as inconsistent with its stated No First Use policy and traditional minimum deterrent posture. It urges China to increase transparency and engage in arms control by answering outstanding questions at the PrepCom.
entries.quote: stated “No First Use” policy and strays from its historic focus on a “minimum deterrent” force structure. China, to date, has resisted calls to explain its build-up or engage on arms control. During this PrepCom, we hope China will abandon its approach of distractions and avoidance and instead demonstrate responsibility by answering the questions many of us have posed over the last two PrepComs.

entries.position_summary: The United States is willing to pursue negotiations in the Conference on Disarmament on effective international arrangements providing security assurances to non-nuclear-weapon states. It also supports commencing negotiations on a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty as the next logical disarmament step and urges leveraging the CD’s work program to advance these talks.
entries.quote: At the same time, and in a spirit of compromise, the United States is prepared to discuss the establishment of an ad hoc committee in the CD to negotiate on effective international arrangements to assure non-nuclear-weapon states against the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons, in conjunction with commencing negotiations on a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty, which we have long viewed as the next logical step in disarmament. With the adoption of a Decision of Work in the CD and the prospect of sustained Subsidiary Body work in 2025, we should take this charge seriously and press forward with creativity and flexibility.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its unequivocal commitment to a world without nuclear weapons and to pursuing general and complete disarmament under effective international controls. It underscores the value of bilateral arms control, notes past reductions, and expresses readiness to engage Russia and China on future frameworks and risk reduction, while warning that Russia’s noncompliance jeopardizes prospects for a post-2026 New START successor. It aligns with the P5 view that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought.
entries.quote: the substance of which we support. 2000 Steps Six and Eleven; 2010 Actions Three and Five: Unequivocal Undertaking to Eliminate Nuclear Arsenals and Reaffirmation of Objective for General and Complete Disarmament: On Steps Six and Eleven, the unequivocal U.S. commitment to the peace and security of a world without nuclear weapons cannot be doubted, and consistent with the 2022 P5 Joint Leaders statement, we recognize that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought. The only way to totally eliminate all nuclear risk is through complete, irreversible, global nuclear disarmament. And we remain committed to the Thirteen Step’s call for pursuing in good faith measures toward general and complete disarmament under strict and effective international controls. 2000 Step Seven; 2010 Action Four: START II, START III, and ABM Treaties: On Step Seven, the 2000 Review Conference spoke to the importance of bilateral arms control agreements. Over the past three decades, the United States has made significant reductions in nuclear weapons – both unilaterally and through arms control agreements with the former Soviet Union and then Russia. The arms control environment has clearly evolved since 2000, and specific topics from this step have been overtaken by developments. We have made clear our readiness to engage on future arms control frameworks with Russia and to work to reduce nuclear risks with the PRC. However, Russia’s noncompliance with existing obligations and outright rejection of arms control dialogue casts a shadow over the likelihood of a New START successor after February 2026. Meanwhile, the

entries.position_summary: The United States argues that Russia and China’s refusal to engage in arms control compels it and its allies to prepare for unconstrained nuclear competition. In such a context, the U.S. may reassess and potentially augment its nuclear capabilities and adjust its posture to address these threats.
entries.quote: However, in contrast to our positive approach and longstanding efforts to manage rivalry and unrestrained competition through arms control, the outright refusal of Russia and the PRC to even discuss arms control at this time obliges the United States and our close allies and partners to prepare for a world of nuclear competition without numerical constraints. In such a world, the United States might have to reconsider its capabilities and posture to account for the threats posed by Russia and the PRC.

entries.position_summary: The country urges that Russia’s substantial non-strategic nuclear weapons be included in arms control agreements with the United States. It also condemns Russia’s reported development of a nuclear-armed antisatellite capability and insists that nuclear weapons remain prohibited in outer space, as reaffirmed by the UN General Assembly.
entries.quote: including its substantial non-strategic nuclear weapon arsenal in arms control agreements with the United States. Russia is also developing a satellite meant to carry a nuclear weapon as an antisatellite capability into outer space – a domain where nuclear weapons are prohibited, and should remain prohibited, as the UN General Assembly reaffirmed overwhelmingly last Fall.

entries.position_summary: The country presses Russia to uphold arms control and pursue risk reduction, rejecting Russia’s purported suspension of New START as legally invalid and condemning its nuclear rhetoric for heightening risks of miscalculation. It also criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear buildup, calling into question Beijing’s transparency and intentions.
entries.quote: We continue to press Russia on arms control and risk reduction despite its legally invalid purported suspension of the New START Treaty and its rhetoric regarding nuclear weapons that not only increase nuclear risks but also heighten the possibility of miscalculation. Meanwhile, China’s rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up raises questions about Beijing’s 2

entries.position_summary: The United States praises the NPT’s success and urges States Parties to use the PrepCom to advance the Treaty through arms control, reducing risks of nuclear conflict, ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and expanding access to peaceful uses of nuclear technology.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States to the NPT Preparatory Committee Statement by Deputy Assistant Secretary of State Gonzalo Suarez, U.S. Department of State, Bureau of International Security and Nonproliferation New York, NY May 9, 2025 Chair, colleagues, In our opening statement last week, the United States praised the remarkable success of this Treaty over the past fifty-five years and reaffirmed that all States Parties continue to benefit greatly from it. We urged all States Parties to use this PrepCom to develop positive ideas that advance the NPT, whether on arms control, reducing the risk of nuclear conflict, ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards, or expanding access to peaceful uses.

entries.position_summary: The country supports engaging China and Russia to pursue verifiable arms control that reduces nuclear conflict risk. It prioritizes developing and partnering on verification technologies and emphasizes creating a security environment conducive to disarmament, including through risk reduction measures.
entries.quote: President Trump has made clear his readiness to engage China and Russia to reduce the risks of nuclear conflict. We will seek verifiable arms control arrangements that enhance American national security. We have pursued diligently the technology necessary to advance future nuclear disarmament treaties, leading a decade of partnership with states to ensure that future disarmament measures can be effectively verified. We also emphasize the importance of creating a security environment conducive to disarmament, including by using risk reduction measures to avoid inadvertent escalation.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges strengthening the NPT and pursuing Article VI-consistent, engagement-based arms control to prevent nuclear arms races, calling specifically on China and Russia to participate. It signals readiness to engage and critiques what it sees as ‘shortcuts’ to disarmament, implicitly rejecting the TPNW approach ahead of the next Review Conference.
entries.quote: As we look toward next year’s Review Conference, we must remember what brought us here decades ago. We are here to advance our collective security by strengthening the NPT, preventing the proliferation of nuclear weapons, and, yes, pursuing their ultimate elimination. The next step is clear, we need China and Russia to work with us to that end. Unlike some efforts that seek shortcuts to nuclear disarmament, it is only through such engagement that we can pursue effective measures to prevent nuclear arms races and make progress towards nuclear disarmament. The United States remains ready to pursue that way forward, and we urge everyone to use your collective voices to demand that these states engage without further 4

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes Russia for developing novel nuclear weapons and enhancing dual-capable delivery systems, signaling expansion and modernization of its arsenal. It also asserts that Russia has refused to return to compliance with the New START Treaty as it nears expiration, resisting arms control constraints.
entries.quote: Russia is developing novel nuclear weapons and improving its suite of dual- capable delivery systems. It has refused to return to compliance with the New START Treaty, which expires in less than a year, and has resisted 2

entries.position_summary: The United States urges China to engage in arms control talks now as part of its NPT/Article VI obligations, rather than waiting until its arsenal reaches a certain size. It signals concern about the growth of China’s nuclear forces and frames early engagement as a test of Beijing’s NPT commitments.
entries.quote: The United States calls on China to act responsibly and engage in these discussions. If China is serious about its NPT commitments, the world should not have to wait until China’s nuclear program reaches a set number for China to engage seriously in arms control discussions.

entries.position_summary: The United States signals readiness to engage in bilateral arms control discussions with Russia and China without preconditions. This reflects openness to dialogue as a means of advancing arms control and reducing strategic risk.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, All this notwithstanding, our National Security Advisor has made clear that the United States is ready to engage in bilateral arms control discussions with Russia and with the PRC, without preconditions.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates for engaging in arms control dialogues with China and Russia to reduce the risk of nuclear conflict and urges States Parties to support these efforts. It dismisses alternative proposals as empty and unverifiable, implicitly criticizing the TPNW.
entries.quote: By contrast, President Trump has made clear his readiness to have arms control dialogues with China and Russia, with the goal of reducing the risks of nuclear conflict. We hope that States Parties will focus their energy on calling on them to do so. Join us in that dialogue instead of echoing empty and unverifiable proposals.

entries.position_summary: The country urges the PRC to engage in substantive strategic nuclear dialogue to prevent miscalculation and miscommunication. It signals support for risk-reduction, transparency, and potential arms control engagement with China.
entries.quote: It is time for the PRC substantively to engage with us on strategic nuclear issues in order to avoid risks of miscalculation and miscommunication.
